The whole kind of thesis behind this is, look, if you go and look at a contact center conversation between an agent and a customer, you'll probably learn a ton of very, very useful stuff.
There might be feedback, customers saying, hey, this thing is too expensive.
This process is too hard.
Hey, I went to your website and I tried to do this and I wasn't able to.

There might be things like the agent.
Asking the wrong or right discovery questions that are really, really relevant to how well or poorly that call goes.
And these are all things you can understand very intuitively when you look at a conversation as a human and you, you know, you kind of read it and you understand things.

When you scale that up to the thousands and thousands and thousands of contact center conversations that are happening in, you know, a contact center like United Airlines or CVS every day, that manual review part becomes completely impractical.
And so you need a tool that's able to understand conversations at this depth, but also at this scale.
And that's kind of what Insights sets out to do.
The way that it sort of works is in these three sort of phases, right?
So we have discovery tools, first of all, kind of unknown unknowns.
Tell me something I don't know about what's happening in my contact center.
Whether that's a topic that's really spiking in the last week or so, whether that's an emerging question that customers are starting to ask.
a you didn't learn about before.

That's what we call unsupervised large language model technology, just runs by itself, analyzes your conversations and shows you things you didn't know based off of that.

You can follow up on that, you can ask natural language questions using kind of a sub feature of the product called AI analyst, which is our natural language chat GPT for your conversations type of tool.

Hey, I want to know about, I want to know why all of my customers in Germany are suddenly really angry about this product that we just launched.
You might've heard that feedback anecdotally from some of your frontline reps.

You can go and set up some very, very specific rules when the conversation is coming from someone in Germany, when anger is detected, when the topic is this product, let's check out what those conversations are like, let's track the sentiment or the customer satisfaction or the resolution rate of those.

It's also something you can use to make wider kind of business decisions.

And if you're Airlines, you want to avoid that call volume entirely because it costs you money.
So they figured out, based on what people were saying in the conversations, that they were running into this friction point in the app.
Fix the friction point in the app and save millions of dollars from the contact center volume that was deflected away from their contact center as a result.
that's what we mean when we say kind of data-driven business decisions alongside the sort of automation with the AI agents, alongside the augmenting of human agents.

What it does is help you to kind of prioritize and spot what's important, right?
We talk about spotting the behaviors and the actions that truly drive the outcomes that you want as a contact center leader.

is actually relating to and driving and affecting these outcomes that you care about and that are going to be the things that get you promoted or not as a contact center leader.

The ability to infer a customer satisfaction score just from conversations instead gives you, first of all, a ton more data, because it's all the conversations, not 1% of a response rate to a survey.

Instantly, instead of waiting several weeks for the surveys to come back and then the data analysis to run on that data.

goes from this stage to this stage to this stage to this stage.

And this one is still kind of a work in progress, but this is what's called quality management.
So on contact center conversations, traditionally, there is a whole team dedicated to quality assurance.
So this team samples calls at random, right?
Say there's 10,000 calls happening.
They might pick 100 of them on a given day, look at them, look at the conversation on one side, look at a scorecard on the other side and say, did the agent do this?
Yes, no.
Did the agent adhere to this, you know, follow the right process steps?
Yes, no.
Did the agent demonstrate empathy?
Yes, no.
And they basically use that data, number one, for kind of a compliance paper trail, right?
Did the agent make all of the regulatory disclosures they were required to do, but also for kind of behavioral stuff, right?
Are the agents adhering to this behavior in the right way?
Are they following the process steps in the right way?
Um, it's a good idea.
It's completely hamstrung by the fact that.
It's done by humans manually reviewing calls, right?
If you can only review probably like 1% of the conversations, the picture that you get is really, really unclear.
It doesn't really reflect reality.
It's very limited in the depth that it can go to.
And so our quality management tool is something that helps to automate that process and help just make it much more useful.
This is a very known quantity among us and most of our competitors.
Almost all of them will have this quality assurance, quality management feature.
But this is the sort of the dichotomy.
So traditionally, it's a small subset of conversations, manually scored, takes about 30 hours of any one person's time per week.
So it's basically a full-time job.
You get at most 2% to 5% coverage of the conversations that are happening.
It's very focused on that compliance paper trail.
The way that it works in our world is, number one, finding what works.
So again, talked about, in the insights part, we talked about finding what are the behaviours that drive resolution, what are the behaviours that drive high CSAT.
If you can figure out what those are, you can make sure that those are the things that you put in your scorecard.
So the scorecard is no longer just like a wishlist of things the executive thinks are important.
The scorecard is now things that we know are important because the data has told us that they're important.
So making sure the scorecard is right, first of all, then using AI to automatically evaluate it across 100% of your conversations.
So it's no longer this small little slice that's subject to sampling bias and errors and all of that.
It's all of the conversations.
They get vetted and calibrated by human analysts to make sure that the AI is behaving the way that we expect it to and scoring things correctly.
What that does is give you that 100% coverage.
It cuts the workload of human agents or human analysts, sorry, in half.

The critical part here is not necessarily replacing those human analysts, but using them to make the system much more efficient, and then also using them to go and coach agents based on the data that comes from it.
If you can imagine trying to coach an agent in the old world, you might have a scorecard on one or two out of every hundred of their calls.

And if you go and tell them, hey, on this call, you didn't disclose this thing properly, but they did disclose it properly on the other 99 calls that you didn't look at, you can imagine that agent's going to be pretty pissed off, right?
It's just not really reflective of reality.
If you can look at all of their conversations, that all of a sudden is a much cleaner picture.
It's much fairer to the agent, and you're able to coach them based on the things that you know that they're doing or not doing and how important those things are.
And so we give you tools that say, hey, this agent is about 20% behind the top performer.
On this behavior of verifying that the customer's device got rebooted.
There's been eight different times this week where she's had an opportunity to exhibit this behavior and she hasn't done it.
You can have a look at those conversations.
You can see for yourself and then you can schedule a one-to-one coaching session with that agent to say, hey, like, doing great.
Here's the thing that I would focus on for next week.
This, you know, verified device reboot here is the places where I would love if you would have done it in your conversations this week.
Let's keep a lookout for those situations next week and let's make sure that we're hitting that behavior and making sure that that device is rebooted.
That's the kind of overall thesis for quality management and coaching.
This is one where it's a lot more mature, right?
There's a lot more customers doing these kinds of things.
Most of them are, they're doing things like reducing the workload of their quality management teams.
They're helping agents to ramp up a little bit faster.
They're increasing what we call span of control.
So for every content.
Center Manager, how many agents can they manage and can they oversee, right?
If you get that clearer picture of each agent's performance, that lets you monitor more agents at a time.

It also lowers agent attrition, boosts employee satisfaction, again, for that same reason of you're giving people coaching that is more reflective of reality and more fair to what's actually going on in their world.
But this is still kind of a work in progress content-wise, but I'll just show you a couple of the differentiators that we tend to have.
Again, the outcomes are the big kind of like head and shoulders above the rest part of it here.
The ability to infer outcomes from a conversation, resolution, sales, customer satisfaction.
That means that the stuff that goes in the scorecard is the stuff that's really important.

It's AI agents as well.
And that's, again, I'll go on a little kind of side tangent here for a second.
In the past, it was pretty easy to monitor and figure out what virtual agents were doing versus what they should have been doing.
Because all of the responses from those virtual agents were pretty hard-coded, deterministic on Rails.
You know what the agent is going to say, like word for word, given a certain set of inputs.
Now, in this new age with large language models and generative AI agents, the determinism is gone.
You no longer can guarantee what the agent will say in response to any one input.
Your AI agents all of a sudden behave and speak like human agents.
And so you can all of a sudden use the same tools that you use to oversee human agents to oversee AI agents as well.
And lucky for you, that's what we've been doing for the last seven, eight years.
So that's a differentiator that we have versus a newer player like Sierra or Decagon who have not spent the last seven, eight years building quality management tools for human agents.
